text: The surgeon demonstrates the port placement for the laparoscopic adrenalectomy on an obese patient. The camera port is positioned pararectally due to the significant distance from the umbilicus, ensuring optimal access to the adrenal gland.
The surgeon places a 12mm air seal port in the pararectal line, positioned two centimeters above the umbilicus, providing a stable pressure environment for the laparoscopic adrenalectomy.

The surgeon places two five millimeter ports in the abdominal wall, indicating the approximate location of the adrenal gland. An additional port may be placed if there are difficulties reaching the adrenal gland from the current positions.
The surgeon navigates the left hand over the camera to interact with the assistant, utilizing two ports for the surgeon, one assisting port, and one camera holder during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the spleen and bowel, then begins a lateral colic incision to access the surgical area.
The surgical team plans to approach the pedicle of the kidney, preparing to navigate through the anatomical structures visible in the laparoscopic view.
The surgeon advances cranially towards the kidney pedicle, aiming to visualize the adrenal gland.
The surgeon identifies the pedicle of the renal artery and vein, using these structures as landmarks to facilitate the identification of vessels during the adrenalectomy.
The surgeon identifies the colon and emphasizes the importance of adequately mobilizing it during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a Carl Storz bipolar instrument, visible in the surgical field, to perform precise tissue manipulation during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon holds a pair of simple scissors in the right hand, preparing to continue the dissection with caution due to the importance of the instrument being used.
The camera view shows a large spleen that requires mobilization, but the surgeon pauses to wait for the bipolar instrument to be connected.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the spleen, preparing it for further manipulation during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesocolon, revealing the underlying kidney, while carefully manipulating the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the kidney and the surrounding mesentery, considering the mobilization of the entire kidney.
The surgeon visually identifies the adrenal gland, highlighting its location and appearance in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the upper pole of the kidney, carefully manipulating the surrounding tissue to expose the adrenal gland.
The surgeon points out the tumor, providing orientation within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the tumor and examines the surrounding vessels to determine the starting point for adrenal gland dissection. There appears to be normal adrenal tissue present, and the surgeon has preserved the adrenal on the opposite side, allowing for a radical adrenalectomy.
The surgeon attempts to locate the pedicle, with Gerota's fascia clearly visible in the surgical field.
On the right edge of the surgical field, the surgeon identifies what appears to be the genital vein, suggesting it could be traced further during the procedure.
The surgeon moves higher in the surgical field, revealing the adrenal gland. A distinct layer is visible around the gland, aiding in orientation.
The surgeon identifies a vessel adjacent to the adrenal gland during the ablative surgery, highlighting the absence of reconstruction required at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the renal vein, noting a branch extending towards the adrenal mass.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, confirming its location during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon encounters a hard tissue area, exercising caution to avoid cutting into the tumor. The lens rotation alters the anatomical orientation, requiring careful navigation.
The surgeon identifies a vein clearly leading to the tumor. The surrounding tissue being pushed down appears hard.
The surgeon requests a Hem-o-lok clip, likely to secure a vessel during the adrenalectomy procedure.

The surgeon applies Hem-o-lok clips to secure the vessels, using medium-sized clips for larger vessels and smaller clips for smaller vessels, ensuring two clips are placed on a specific vessel for added security.
The surgeon gains first access to the inferior region, revealing the surface of the kidney adjacent to the tumor site.

The surgeon encounters a large vessel and proceeds with further mobilization to identify it, suspecting it might be an upper pole vessel to the kidney. The vessel appears larger than typical adrenal vessels, prompting cautious handling.
The remaining kidney is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon prepares to open the area, discussing different approaches to access the tumor.
The surgeon identifies minor vessels within the surgical field and uses an instrument to cut them without difficulty.
The surgeon points to the tumor, which has been mobilized from its surrounding tissue, providing a clearer view of its location.
The surgeon demonstrates the importance of adequately mobilizing the spleen to access the surgical area, adjusting the camera view to provide a closer look at the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon points out the entire tumor, contrasting it with the healthy part of the adrenal gland. The metastasis is visually identified adjacent to the tumor.
The camera view shows the patient in a lateral position, providing a clear view of the kidney and the pedicle, indicating a favorable access point for the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the artery, exercising caution while navigating around it within the surgical field.
The surgical field reveals a significant amount of adipose tissue. The surgeon is attempting to identify a distinct anatomical layer within the fat.
The surgeon identifies the psoas muscle as a landmark, with the kidney visible nearby. The anatomy is clearly defined, indicating that most of the surgical dissection is complete.
The surgeon identifies lymph fluid in the surgical field, with the adrenal gland clearly visible alongside surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the medial vein of the adrenal gland and applies a Hem-o-lok clip to secure it.
The surgeon attempts to rotate the adrenal tumor, revealing remaining adhesions attached to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon employs a no-touch technique, carefully maneuvering around the adrenal tumor to avoid direct contact and prevent mobilization of tumor cells.
The surgeon identifies the healthy adrenal gland and the main mass of the tumor on the opposite side, preparing to use an endoback device.
The surgeon requests a second pair of forceps, indicating the use of these instruments in the ongoing procedure.
